There's just really three things I
wanna talk to the committee about.
First of all, um, this bill is an attack on free speech.
This government says that it cares about free speech.
In particular, the ACT Party has been a leading campaigner
against hate speech laws.
I dare say that if the labor government had attempted
to pass this law three years ago,
there would've been a very loud act led opposition to it.
Act would say that limits on free speech must only be
that which are absolutely necessary in a free
and democratic society.
Yet funnily enough, these absolutely necessary limitations
conveniently align with the government's agenda on gang
patches, foreign interference, and in this case protest.
But such limitations are an unbearable
outreach akin to fascism.
When what is in question are the rights of people not
to be subjected to endless, violent, racist hate,
homophobia and misogyny.
True believers in free speech shouldn't fall back on
legislation to silence their political opponents under the
guise of protecting privacy
when it is politically convenient.
I wanna provide you a real life example of
how this will actually silence people.
Over the past three years in Wellington, we have had dozens
of demonstrations outside the Iranian embassy.
And Tai, this is an ordinary residence on an ordinary
residential street.
These protests have been noisy, they have been inconvenient,
they have been disruptive.
They have stood against the repressive theocratic Iranian
regime and its murdering of political dissidents.
These have been led by the Iranian New Zealand community,
people who have fled to come here
where they hope their right to freedom
of speech is protected.
Yet it is this very community
that would be dissuaded from protesting by this
who are already fearful of state forces
and who would likely not go there if they thought they might
be summarily arrested without even a
warning for holding a protest.
This limitation on free speech is not reasonable
and is proportionate
and it should not be excused by those
who claim to fight for free.
Speech Number two, this bill is open to the worst kind
of arbitrary state overreach.
This bill is set up for arbitrary and
therefore unjust application by the police.
What might be okay in Central Auckland on 9:00 PM on a
Tuesday night would be very different to
what is okay in dip in at 9:00 PM on a Tuesday night.
Indeed, what might be okay to one police sergeant
one day might not be okay to one another on the next.
So instead of providing clarity
and uniformity to police,
this introduces deliberate arbitrariness and to the law.
Instead of having the confidence in and z Bora
and the police, the government is seeking to legislate when,
as I note in my submission, police already have a range
of tools including arresting people for breach of the peace.
Police are being asked to apply a complicated legal test
and balancing act while out on the streets, out on the beat
to decide what is unreasonable in truth.
It is not reasonable to ask them to do so.
I should know, I went through the district court,
the high court, the Court of Appeal
and the Supreme Court over a period of four years
to establish the important free speech precedent
in Morse versus police.
The highest court in the land with the benefit of facts
and evidence decided what was reasonable,
what was offensive, and what was disorderly.
It is also completely unreasonable to have no trigger
for arrest in this law.
It is unreasonable to have roadworks
outside your house at 6:00 AM
but do police come and arrest the road workers?
No. An atheist would see the singing
of Christmas carols on Christmas Eve
outside his house as unreasonable.
But do we arrest carolers instead
of simply asking them to leave?
And your next door neighbor's, 21st party
with 200 people in a live band all day
on Sunday is unreasonable.
But does team policing roll in and start mass arrests? No.
So why does political speech have a lower threshold
and a lower tolerance than anything else?
This is finally, this is unnecessary and
therefore bad lawmaking.
The National Party says that since 1938, quote,
our values have not just been words on a page.
One of those values you say is quote limited government.

because something that rarely happens has disturbed Erica
Stanford and Winston Peters.

no legs to stand on.
It makes clear that this law has very marginal effect.
So the question must be why pass it?
This is ironically the kind of nanny state intervention

accused its opposition of doing.
It is virtue signaling pure and simple.

Yes, there are occasional protest outside MPS homes

Um, I've been concerned about the lack of time allowed
for consultation on this bill.
There's been no adequate consultation with Maori,
with legal experts and with the public.
Um, and again, the impact statement points out
that there's very limited data on the number
and impact of the type of demonstrations
that would fall into this, this category of offense.
Um, so there's very little data on the need for the bill
or the problems associated with enforcing it.
Um, and I, I think
that it would place police officers in a very difficult
situation of having to interpret difficult, having
to interpret vague definitions of disruption
and newness, um, during possibly volatile events, you know,
leading to the potential for discriminatory
and inconsistent decision making.
Police do have a very complex job, um,
and the legislation that they enforce needs to be robust.
Um, and this is not it, it is, there are too many vague
criteria there that really put police officers at risk
of error or even exacerbating a difficult situation.
Um, being able to arrest without a previous warning,
you know, is, is a, um, yeah,
is is giving the police a degree
of responsibility without a safety, um,
which is very difficult.
And the penalties that can be imposed three months in prison
or $2,000 fine, they're very severe considering
that people may not even be aware
that they're breaking the law.
So, and again, the criteria for courts
to decide whether the demonstration is unreasonable
or not are also vague
and it's impossible to be certain
what would be considered against the law without
lengthy argument.
Um, which could just tie up the courts
and compared with present legislation,
which can be used very effectively.
So, um, the definition of residential premises,
as somebody else just pointed out, is, is very vague
and it could be spread to almost anywhere
that would normally be considered appropriate for a scene
of a, a protest like an embassy,
for example, or the streets.
So I believe the right
to protest is a vital pillar of our democracy.
And because this bill risks criminalizing legitimate
protest, it will have a chilling effect on protest overall,
which is an attack on a healthy democracy.
Protest has been the catalyst
for social development many times throughout the history
of Artie r New Zealand.
And the right to protest must be protected.
Public pressure has given us many examples of success
after initial opposition and,
and we've shown ourselves to be on the right side of history
opposing apartheid and, and opposing nuclear testing.
Any government facing public protests should ask itself
what is happening in society to provoke this dissatisfaction
and how this can be addressed.
The climate, crisis, poverty, low pay, poor services,
they're all cause of protest
and they should be taken seriously.
Criminalizing protest is not a responsible solution
and it will not provide answers to the problems faced
by protestors.
Protest draws attention to the needs of communities
and societies and criminalizing protests is a sign
that a government is not taking responsibility
for meeting those needs.
So I'm asking you please to reject this bill. Thank you.
Well, thank you Jean,
and I'm glad you joined us from the west coast.
Is it fruitful down there?
It's raining, but it's actually quite warm.
I, I am from Otta Porte, uh, Dunedin.
Um, and it's warmer over here, but definitely wetter.
Right. Thanks.
Um, thank you for your submission this morning.
We've run out of time, unfortunately,
but hey, really appreciate you joining
us and making that effort.
Well, you are welcome. Thank you.
Okay, thank, thank you everyone.
Um, now we're just going back into private for a second
and then we'll, um, you're about to get into the house.
Okay. All.
